key_insight: In multiobjective topology optimization, the Pareto front can be disconnected and nonconvex because it is composed of pieces of local fronts, and scalarization choice strongly affects how well it is captured.
engineering_relevance: For practical design trade-offs in structures, choosing a stronger scalarization method can yield more uniform and representative Pareto approximations across objectives such as compliance, stress, volume, and dynamic compliance.
method: Compared weighted-sum, ε-constraint, and Pascoletti-Serafini scalarizations.
Developed theoretical comparisons of the three approaches for bi-objective topology optimization.
Ran multiple numerical bi-objective examples to assess front coverage and clustering behavior.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports a large numerical study with many bi-objective topology optimization examples, showing more uniform Pareto approximations for Pascoletti-Serafini and clustering for weighted-sum.
watch_item: Pareto fronts may be locally fragmented and nonconvex, so a single scalarization or coarse sampling can miss important design trade-offs.
paper_title: Novel insights into Pareto fronts in multiobjective topology optimization and a comparative study of scalarization strategies
